fix_methods: Output encoding/escaping per context; Use safe templating with auto-escaping; Strict input validation/whitelisting; Sanitize HTML using well-maintained libraries; Content Security Policy (CSP) with script-src nonce/hashes; HttpOnly cookies for sensitive cookies; CSP report-uri for monitoring; Use frameworks' built-in protections; Apply WAF rules for XSS signatures; CSP + Subresource Integrity (SRI) for third-party scripts
recommended_fix_short: Escape all user-supplied data at output, sanitize stored HTML if needed, and enforce a strong CSP.

fix_methods: Use parameterized queries / prepared statements; Use ORM query builders that parameterize inputs; Input validation & whitelists for identifiers; Avoid dynamic SQL string concatenation; Least-privilege DB user; Limit returned columns and rows; Use query parameter binding and stored procedures (careful with dynamic SQL inside); Database activity monitoring & WAF signatures; Escape only when absolutely necessary and with safe libraries
recommended_fix_short: Replace concatenated SQL with parameterized queries and enforce input validation and least-privilege DB access.

fix_methods: Use per-session/per-request CSRF tokens and validate server-side; Set SameSite=strict or lax on auth cookies; Verify Origin and Referer headers for state-changing requests; Double-submit cookie pattern when necessary; Require explicit user interaction for critical actions (re-auth or 2FA); Use anti-CSRF libraries/framework middleware; Avoid GET for state-changing operations
recommended_fix_short: Require a validated CSRF token for all state-changing requests and use SameSite cookie attributes.

fix_methods: Enforce server-side authorization checks for every resource access; Use unguessable identifiers (UUIDs or random tokens) not sequential IDs; Map user-owned resources in server-side ACLs; Principle of least privilege; Validate ownership/permissions on every operation; Logging and alerting for access anomalies; Rate-limiting on resource access attempts
recommended_fix_short: Always authorize on the server for requested resources and avoid predictable IDs.

fix_methods: Avoid executing shell commands with user input; Use language-native APIs instead of shell; If shell needed, use exec APIs that accept argument arrays (no shell interpolation); Strict input validation and allowlists; Drop privileges before executing; Use chroot/containers/sandboxing; Use seccomp/AppArmor/SELinux policies; Escape arguments only with robust libraries; Use timeout and resource limits
recommended_fix_short: Replace shell invocations with safe APIs and strictly whitelist/validate any required inputs.

fix_methods: Avoid native/object deserialization of untrusted data; Use simple data formats (JSON) with schema validation; Use allowlists for allowed types; Sign/encrypt serialized payloads and verify signatures before deserialization; Run deserialization in isolated/sandboxed processes; Keep deserialization libraries up to date; Use safe parsers that prohibit executable types
recommended_fix_short: Don't deserialize untrusted data; prefer validated JSON and verify integrity before parsing.

fix_methods: Sanitize input before storage (remove scripts/unsafe tags) using vetted libraries; Escape on output according to context; Use Content Security Policy; Validate input length and allowed markup; Use stored-html sanitizers that preserve safe tags (if needed); Audit stored content rendering paths; WAF rules for stored payload patterns
recommended_fix_short: Sanitize and/or encode stored user content and enforce CSP.

fix_methods: Only allow internal redirects or use a server-side allowlist of domains; Use a token or key that maps to an approved URL instead of accepting full user-supplied URLs; Validate that redirect targets are relative or on the same host; Disallow full URLs from user input in redirect parameters; Log and monitor redirect usage
recommended_fix_short: Validate redirect targets against an allowlist or use internal mapping keys.

fix_methods: Validate file type by magic bytes and content analysis; Enforce allowlist of MIME types; Store uploads outside webroot and serve via controlled handlers; Sanitize filenames and generate server-side filenames; Set safe filesystem permissions (no execute); Scan files for malware; Limit file size and rate; Use content-disposition and Content-Type checks; Use object storage with fine-grained access controls
recommended_fix_short: Validate and sanitize uploads, store them safely outside the webroot, and scan for malicious content.

fix_methods: Use strong password hashing algorithms (bcrypt/argon2/PBKDF2) with salt and appropriate cost; Use per-user salts and consider a server-side pepper; Migrate weak hashes safely; Enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA); Implement account lockout/rate-limiting on login attempts; Use secure session management and rotate session on privilege changes; Secure password reset flows with short-lived tokens and verification
recommended_fix_short: Store passwords with strong hashing (argon2/bcrypt), enforce MFA and rate limits on auth flows.

fix_methods: Use TLS (HTTPS) for all transport and enforce HSTS; Encrypt sensitive fields at rest with proper key management; Rotate keys and use KMS or HSM; Minimize data collection and retention; Tokenize or redact PII in logs; Use field-level encryption for highly sensitive data; Audit access to secrets and use secret management solutions
recommended_fix_short: Encrypt data in transit and at rest, use proper key management and minimize stored sensitive data.

fix_methods: Set a strict allowlist of origins; Never echo request Origin blindly; Configure Access-Control-Allow-Credentials correctly; Use preflight checks and minimize exposed methods/headers; Use libraries/framework features to manage CORS; Set Vary: Origin header to avoid caching issues; Restrict endpoints that accept credentials
recommended_fix_short: Restrict CORS to a specific origin allowlist and avoid reflecting the Origin header.

fix_methods: Implement rate limits per-IP and per-account; Use exponential backoff and progressive delays; Apply burst limits and daily caps for expensive ops; Use CAPTCHA for suspicious flows; Throttle or queue expensive background jobs; Alert on unusual spikes; Combine with WAF for bot detection
recommended_fix_short: Apply rate limiting and progressive throttling on auth and expensive endpoints.

fix_methods: Normalize and canonicalize paths; Reject input containing ../ sequences after normalization; Map user requests to a safe filename map or identifiers; Use platform-safe path APIs (no manual string joins); Serve files from a chrooted or dedicated directory; Enforce allowlist of accessible files; Use safe file-serve middleware
recommended_fix_short: Canonicalize and validate file paths; map to allowed filenames or store files outside the webroot.

fix_methods: Disable debug endpoints in production builds; Protect with strong authentication and IP allowlist; Require MFA for admin actions; Use feature flags or runtime checks to hide endpoints; Monitor and alert on accesses; Remove verbose error messages
recommended_fix_short: Remove or restrict debug endpoints in production and require strong auth for admin interfaces.

fix_methods: Log authentication failures, privilege changes, and data access; Ensure logs are tamper-evident and centralized (SIEM); Redact PII in logs; Configure alerting for important events; Retain logs for an appropriate retention period; Test detection and response runbooks
recommended_fix_short: Implement centralized, secure logging and alerting for security-relevant events.

fix_methods: Set HttpOnly on session cookies to prevent JS access; Set Secure so cookies only sent over HTTPS; Use SameSite=strict or lax depending on needs; Set appropriate Path and Domain; Rotate session IDs on login/logout; Use short cookie expiration for sensitive sessions
recommended_fix_short: Set Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite attributes and rotate session identifiers.

fix_methods: Regularly scan dependencies for known CVEs (SCA tools); Keep dependencies up to date; Use dependency allowlists and pin versions; Apply vulnerability patching policies and test updates in CI; Use SBOM and monitor dependency advisories; Limit privileges of libraries (e.g., run in restricted environment)
recommended_fix_short: Continuously monitor and patch dependencies; maintain an SBOM and automated scanning.

fix_methods: Use strict server-side validation and typed schemas (JSON Schema, protobuf); Prefer whitelists over blacklists; Validate length, format, ranges; Normalize inputs early; Fail securely with clear errors; Use input parsing libraries that protect against edge cases; Use fuzzing in QA
recommended_fix_short: Validate all inputs server-side using whitelists and schemas; normalize before use.

fix_methods: Disable TLS 1.0/1.1 and weak ciphers; Prefer TLS 1.3 or TLS 1.2 with secure ciphers; Enable HSTS; Use modern certificate management and automate renewal; Enable OCSP stapling; Regularly scan with tls configuration tools; Use forward secrecy ciphers
recommended_fix_short: Harden TLS configuration, enable HSTS, and use modern ciphers with automated certificate management.

fix_methods: Never use user-supplied input directly in file path functions; Implement strict allowlist of permitted files/paths; Use realpath() to resolve and validate paths before use; Ensure resolved path starts with expected base directory; Disable allow_url_include in PHP (php.ini); Use basename() to strip directory traversal sequences; Apply chroot or containerization to limit file system access; Log and alert on path traversal attempts; Use indirect file references (map keys to file names server-side); WAF rules to detect ../ and encoded variants
recommended_fix_short: Validate and canonicalize file paths against a strict allowlist; never pass raw user input to file-include functions.

fix_methods: Disable allow_url_include and allow_url_fopen in PHP configuration; Never include or require files from user-supplied URLs; Use static file references or an allowlist of internal file paths only; Enforce egress firewall rules to block outbound HTTP from the app server; Input validation and strict allowlist for any file parameter; Use Content Security Policy; Containerize and restrict filesystem mounts; Monitor and alert on unexpected outbound connections from web server; WAF rules to block RFI payloads
recommended_fix_short: Disable PHP remote URL includes, use only internal allowlisted paths, and block outbound connections from the web server.

fix_methods: Avoid eval(), exec(), system(), shell_exec() with user input; Use safe language-native APIs instead of shell commands; Apply strict input validation and allowlisting; Sandbox application execution environment (containers, VMs, seccomp); Enforce least-privilege OS user for the app process; Apply WAF rules for known RCE payloads; Keep all libraries and interpreters patched; Use RASP (Runtime Application Self-Protection) tools; Disable dangerous PHP functions via disable_functions; Implement network segmentation to limit blast radius; Monitor process spawning from web server processes
recommended_fix_short: Never execute user-supplied data as code; use safe APIs, sandbox the app, and keep dependencies patched.

fix_methods: Never pass user input directly into template rendering functions; Use logic-less or sandboxed template engines where possible (e.g., Mustache); If using Jinja2, use sandbox mode; Validate and escape all user input before it reaches the template engine; Separate template code from data; Use allowlists for template variables; Apply WAF rules for template expression syntax ({{ }}, ${}, etc.); Code review all template rendering paths; Run template engine in isolated process with limited privileges
recommended_fix_short: Never render user input as a template; use sandboxed engines and validate all template inputs strictly.

fix_methods: Disable external entity processing in XML parsers (FEATURE_SECURE_PROCESSING, setFeature flags); Use JSON instead of XML where possible; Use allowlisted, hardened XML libraries; Disable DOCTYPE declarations if not required; Apply input validation to XML content; Use a WAF to filter XXE payloads; Scan for XXE in SAST/DAST pipelines; Do not process XML from untrusted sources without strict controls; Apply network egress controls to prevent out-of-band data exfiltration
recommended_fix_short: Disable external entity and DOCTYPE processing in XML parsers; prefer JSON over XML for untrusted data.

fix_methods: Validate and allowlist target URLs/hostnames before making server-side requests; Block requests to private IP ranges (127.x, 10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x); Use a dedicated egress proxy that enforces allowlists; Disable redirects or validate redirect targets; Resolve and revalidate DNS on each request to prevent DNS rebinding; Avoid passing user-supplied URLs to HTTP client libraries; Use network segmentation and egress firewall rules; Monitor and alert on unexpected internal requests; Apply WAF rules for SSRF payloads; Avoid metadata service endpoints being accessible (cloud IMDS)
recommended_fix_short: Allowlist permitted request targets, block private/internal IPs, and route outbound requests through a controlled egress proxy.

fix_methods: Canonicalize paths with realpath() before any access check; Reject inputs containing ../, ..\ or URL-encoded variants (%2e%2e); Chroot or containerize the application file access; Use indirect references (map keys to file paths server-side); Apply OS-level read permissions limiting the app user; Log and alert on traversal attempts; Validate file extensions against an allowlist after canonicalization; WAF rules for path traversal patterns; Test with multiple encoding variants (double URL-encoding, Unicode normalization)
recommended_fix_short: Always canonicalize and validate paths server-side; reject traversal sequences and confine access to an expected base directory.

fix_methods: Avoid using user-supplied keys to set properties on objects without validation; Use Object.create(null) for dictionaries to avoid prototype chain; Use safe deep-merge libraries (lodash >= 4.17.21); Validate and sanitize merge/clone operations; Use JSON Schema validation before object operations; Apply eslint security plugins to detect prototype pollution patterns; Use Object.freeze(Object.prototype) in test environments; Keep dependencies updated; Prefer Map/Set over plain objects for user-keyed data
recommended_fix_short: Validate merge keys, use null-prototype objects for dictionaries, and keep deep-merge libraries updated.

fix_methods: Audit DNS records regularly and remove dangling CNAMEs pointing to deprovisioned services; Claim or release subdomains before removing cloud resources (S3, GitHub Pages, Heroku, etc.); Use automated tools (subjack, nuclei) to scan for dangling subdomains; Implement DNS monitoring and alerting; Apply strict CSP on parent domain; Use HTTPS everywhere to reduce session hijacking from taken-over subdomains; Maintain an inventory of all active subdomains and their backing services
recommended_fix_short: Audit DNS for dangling CNAMEs, remove or claim subdomains before deprovisioning services.

fix_methods: Set X-Frame-Options: DENY or SAMEORIGIN headers; Use Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors directive (preferred over X-Frame-Options); Implement frame-busting JavaScript as a defense-in-depth measure; Audit all sensitive pages for framing protection; Test with browser developer tools to verify headers are applied; Use security header scanners (securityheaders.com) in CI/CD pipeline
recommended_fix_short: Apply X-Frame-Options and CSP frame-ancestors headers to prevent your pages from being embedded in iframes.

fix_methods: Use HTTP/2 end-to-end where possible (eliminates CL/TE ambiguity); Configure frontend and backend servers to use the same parsing standard; Disable support for ambiguous Transfer-Encoding headers; Use HTTP/1.1 normalization at the reverse proxy; Keep web servers and proxies (nginx, HAProxy, Apache) fully patched; Test with Burp Suite's HTTP Request Smuggler extension; Use strict HTTP parsing in all components; Monitor for anomalous 400/500 responses indicative of desync
recommended_fix_short: Use HTTP/2 end-to-end and ensure frontend/backend agree on Transfer-Encoding handling; keep proxies patched.

fix_methods: Use allowlists (strong parameters, @JsonProperty allowlists) to explicitly permit only expected fields; Never bind raw request bodies directly to model objects without filtering; Use DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) to map request data to business objects; Review all model binding configurations; Apply SAST rules to detect mass assignment patterns; Audit API endpoints that accept JSON bodies; Use framework-provided protection (Rails strong_parameters, Spring @JsonIgnore, etc.)
recommended_fix_short: Use allowlist-based parameter binding (DTOs or strong parameters) and never auto-bind raw request data to models.

fix_methods: Threat-model business workflows during design; Enforce server-side state machine validation for multi-step processes; Validate all business rule constraints server-side (price, quantity, discount); Apply rate limiting and anomaly detection on business-critical flows; Use immutable audit logs for financial or critical transactions; Test edge cases (negative values, zero, max int, step skipping) in QA; Implement 4-eyes or approval workflows for high-value actions; Monitor for unusual transaction patterns
recommended_fix_short: Enforce all business rules server-side, validate workflow state transitions, and monitor for anomalous transaction patterns.

fix_methods: Explicitly validate the alg header and reject 'none'; Use a strong, random secret (>=256 bits) for HMAC; Prefer RS256/ES256 (asymmetric) for distributed systems; Validate iss, aud, exp, nbf claims strictly; Use short token expiry and implement refresh token rotation; Never trust unverified JWT payload data; Use well-maintained JWT libraries and avoid custom implementations; Implement token revocation via JTI denylist for high-security flows; Keep signing keys rotated and stored securely in a secrets manager
recommended_fix_short: Reject 'none' algorithm, use strong keys, validate all claims (exp/aud/iss), and prefer asymmetric signing.

vuln_type: Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA/IDOR in APIs)
fix_methods: Enforce ownership/authorization checks on every API endpoint, not just UI; Use UUID instead of sequential IDs for resources; Implement object-level permission middleware; Log and alert on authorization failures; Use API gateways with built-in authz policies; Test each API endpoint with tokens from different users; Apply OWASP API Security Top 10 checks in DAST pipeline; Never rely on obscurity (unguessable IDs) as the sole protection; Use field-level access controls in GraphQL
recommended_fix_short: Enforce server-side ownership checks on every API object access; use UUIDs and test cross-user access in QA.

fix_methods: Upgrade Log4j to 2.17.1+ (Java 8), 2.12.4+ (Java 7), or 2.3.2+ (Java 6); Set log4j2.formatMsgNoLookups=true as an interim mitigation; Remove JndiLookup class from classpath as emergency workaround; Block outbound LDAP/RMI at network level; Scan classpath for vulnerable Log4j versions with tools (logpresso, syft); Apply WAF rules for ${jndi: patterns; Audit all Java dependencies for transitive Log4j usage; Monitor for JNDI callback attempts in network logs
recommended_fix_short: Upgrade Log4j to 2.17.1+, set formatMsgNoLookups=true, block outbound LDAP/RMI, and scan all Java apps for vulnerable versions.

fix_methods: Upgrade Spring Framework to 5.3.18+ or 5.2.20+; Upgrade Spring Boot to 2.6.6+ or 2.5.12+; Upgrade to Tomcat 10.0.20+, 9.0.62+, or 8.5.78+; Requires JDK 9+ to be exploitable — JDK 8 deployments less affected; Apply WAF rules for class.module.classLoader patterns in request parameters; Scan all Spring-based applications for affected versions; Verify update via dependency scanning in CI/CD
recommended_fix_short: Upgrade Spring Framework to 5.3.18+/5.2.20+ and Tomcat to patched versions; block class.module.classLoader parameter access.

fix_methods: Apply Microsoft Exchange emergency patches (March 2021 CUs) immediately; Run Microsoft's MSERT / Safety Scanner to detect IOCs; Check for webshells in Exchange directories; Audit Exchange logs for exploitation indicators; Restrict Exchange access to required network ranges; Enable Extended Protection for Authentication; Implement network segmentation for Exchange servers; Rotate credentials for any accounts that touched affected servers; Review and remove any unauthorized mailbox rules or forwarding
recommended_fix_short: Apply March 2021 Exchange patches immediately, scan for webshells and IOCs, and restrict network access to Exchange.

fix_methods: Update Bash to patched version (>= 4.3 patch 25); Audit all CGI scripts for environment variable injection; Disable CGI where not strictly necessary; Apply WAF rules for Shellshock payload patterns () { :;}; in headers; Scan DHCP, CGI, OpenSSH ForceCommand, Git hooks for exposure; Use process isolation for CGI execution; Apply egress filtering to limit data exfiltration; Review all services that invoke bash with environment variables from user input
recommended_fix_short: Patch Bash to 4.3-25+, disable unnecessary CGI, and apply WAF rules for Shellshock patterns.

fix_methods: Upgrade OpenSSL to 1.0.1g or later; Revoke and reissue all TLS certificates that were served by vulnerable instances; Rotate all private keys; Invalidate all session tokens and force user re-authentication; Change all passwords that may have been exposed; Audit services using OpenSSL (web servers, VPNs, mail servers, load balancers); Enable Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) cipher suites to limit future impact; Apply network monitoring for anomalous TLS traffic patterns
recommended_fix_short: Upgrade OpenSSL to 1.0.1g+, reissue all certificates, rotate private keys, and force user re-authentication.

fix_methods: Apply Microsoft patch MS17-010 on all Windows systems; Disable SMBv1 entirely (it is legacy and should not be used); Block TCP ports 445 and 139 at perimeter and between network segments; Apply network segmentation to limit lateral movement; Enable Windows Firewall with strict rules; Scan network for SMBv1-enabled hosts using nmap; Implement endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions; Monitor for unusual SMB traffic patterns; Ensure Windows Update is enabled and current
recommended_fix_short: Apply MS17-010 immediately, disable SMBv1, and block port 445 at perimeter and between segments.

fix_methods: Upgrade Log4j to 2.17.0+ (addresses infinite recursion DoS); Separate from Log4Shell fix — both CVEs must be addressed; Apply same general Log4j hardening (remove JndiLookup, formatMsgNoLookups); Implement application-level rate limiting and timeouts; Monitor for CPU spikes and thread exhaustion in application metrics; Scan for all Log4j versions in all deployed JARs including shaded/uber JARs
recommended_fix_short: Upgrade Log4j to 2.17.0+ to fix infinite recursion DoS (distinct from Log4Shell; both must be patched).

fix_methods: Disable introspection in production environments; Implement query depth limiting (max 5-10 levels); Apply query complexity analysis and reject high-complexity queries; Rate limit GraphQL endpoints per user/IP; Use query allowlisting (persisted queries) in high-security apps; Enforce field-level authorization; Validate and sanitize all input variables; Avoid exposing sensitive fields in the schema; Use batching limits to prevent batch-based brute force; Apply OWASP GraphQL Cheat Sheet; Monitor query patterns for abuse
recommended_fix_short: Disable production introspection, enforce depth/complexity limits, rate limit, and apply field-level authorization.

vuln_type: Exposed API Keys / Secrets in Source Code
fix_methods: Never hardcode secrets in source code; Use environment variables or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault); Add .gitignore rules for .env files; Use pre-commit hooks with tools like git-secrets, detect-secrets, or truffleHog; Scan Git history for secrets with tools (gitleaks, truffleHog); Rotate any exposed credentials immediately; Use short-lived tokens and IRSA/Workload Identity where possible; Enable secret scanning in GitHub/GitLab Advanced Security; Audit third-party integrations for hardcoded credentials
recommended_fix_short: Store secrets in a secrets manager, never in code; scan Git history for leaks and rotate any exposed credentials immediately.

vuln_type: Misconfigured Cloud Storage (Public S3 / Blob Exposure)
fix_methods: Enable Block Public Access settings on all S3 buckets / Azure Blob containers; Audit bucket ACLs and policies regularly; Use bucket policy conditions to restrict access by VPC, IP, or IAM role; Enable S3 Object Ownership to enforce ACL controls; Use AWS Config / Azure Policy for continuous compliance checks; Enable CloudTrail / Azure Monitor for bucket access logging; Apply least-privilege IAM policies for all roles accessing storage; Use pre-signed URLs for time-limited access instead of public ACLs; Scan for public buckets with cloud security posture management (CSPM) tools
recommended_fix_short: Enable Block Public Access, audit ACLs/policies, restrict with IAM and VPC conditions, and use CSPM for continuous monitoring.
